name: Hot Module Replacement
summary: Build-tool mechanism in JavaScript bundlers (Vite, webpack, esbuild, Parcel) that swaps changed module instances into a running browser application without page reload, preserving component state via runtime cooperation primitives like import.meta.hot.accept.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
Hot Module Replacement (HMR) is a development-time technique for bundlers and dev servers in the JavaScript ecosystem: when a source file changes, the dev server pushes only the changed module to the running browser application, which swaps the new module instance in place of the old one *without* a full page reload. Component state, scroll position, form input, and live WebSocket connections are preserved across the swap — provided every module along the dependency chain has indicated it can accept the update.

Distinct from runtime hot-reload mechanisms in production (`data/erlang-hot-reload`, `data/julia-revise`, `data/dotnet-edit-and-continue`, `data/lisp-incremental-redefinition`, `data/dlopen-live-reload`): HMR is a development-experience concern, not a production code-replacement strategy. It runs in dev servers, depends on bundler-specific protocols, and is not generally safe to deploy to production.

sections.title: import.meta.hot — the runtime cooperation primitive
Module-system preconditions
sections.content:
HMR is feasible because the JavaScript module system has the right preconditions:

- **Stable module identity** — a URL or file path is a deterministic key for swapping.
- **Individually loadable artifacts** — ESM modules are independent records, not flattened into one binary.
- **Live bindings** — ESM importers see the exporter's binding slot, so swapping the slot updates downstream consumers.
- **First-class runtime cooperation** — `import.meta.hot` is part of the runtime, not just a build-tool concept.

Languages that lack one of these (statically-linked binaries, modules-as-singleton-objects with copied exports, no runtime introspection) cannot easily support HMR. The lesson generalises: **a module system that wants HMR must declare runtime cooperation primitives explicitly**.

sections.title: Production implementations
sections.content:
**Vite** (Evan You, 2020) is the most influential modern HMR implementation, built around native ESM in dev mode and Rollup in build mode. **webpack** (Tobias Koppers, 2012) shipped HMR as the original popular implementation; its `module.hot.accept` API predates `import.meta.hot` and remains supported. **esbuild** offers HMR via plugins. **Parcel** ships HMR as a default. **Turbopack** (Vercel, Tobias Koppers's successor to webpack) has HMR as a first-class concern.

Framework-level HMR (React Fast Refresh, Vue HMR, Svelte HMR) sits one layer above bundler HMR: framework-aware updates preserve component state across module swaps, going beyond the bundler's generic accept/dispose hooks. Status (as of 2026-04): React Fast Refresh and Vue HMR are de facto standards; framework integration is what makes HMR feel seamless rather than mechanical.
